text: Mercury ion (Hg2+) transport mechanisms in human gut have not yet been fully elucidated. Current studies on Hg2+ transport generally rely on conventional animal experiments and static cell culture models, which do not well replicate the in vivo intestinal microenvironment. This study presents a gut-on-a-chip platform with integrated transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) and electrochemical sensors. It was applied to investigate the Hg2+ transport within the developed gut barrier model under the stimulation of fluid shear stress (0.02 dyne/cm²) and cyclic mechanical strain (1%, 0.15 Hz). An increase in the tensile strain from 1% to 5% was shown to increase the uptake of Hg2+ by 23.59% and this increased uptake was found to be consistent with physical damage to Caco-2 cell barrier measured by TEER, as well as an enhanced expression of mechanosensitive ion channel Piezo1 and the metal transporter DMT1. The developed gut-on-a-chip platform will greatly facilitate the research on heavy metal toxicity and the mechanobiology of intestinal transport.
